topic: Launching the $97 Always Reviewed Always Ranked playbook
text:
Most law firms know they need more reviews. None of them have a system.

The pattern is the same every time. Someone sends a few requests after a big case. A few reviews trickle in. Momentum dies inside a month. Six months later, the firm's review velocity is flat, rankings slip, and a competitor with half the legal talent owns the Map Pack.

The bottleneck isn't the request. It's the system. Who asks. When they ask. What they say. What happens when a 1-star review shows up on a Tuesday morning. None of that is solved by paying $60–$200 a month for reputation management software — the software automates a process the firm still hasn't actually written down.

So we wrote it down. Eight chapters, seven appendices, 20 response templates (10 positive, 10 negative), the 3-touch request sequence, the monthly velocity scorecard, and the bar-compliant language that keeps you out of trouble with Google and your state bar. $97. One-time. Built for firms that want to keep the system inside the firm.

#LawFirmMarketing #GoogleReviews #LegalMarketing

topic: Launching the $97 Always Found Always Local playbook
text:
Most law firms pay $3K–$10K a month for local SEO. We put the entire playbook in a $97 PDF.

Local search is winner-take-all. Three firms show up in the Map Pack for "personal injury lawyer near me." The fourth one might as well be invisible. There's no participation trophy for ranking #11.

The playbook is what we run for clients — written out in plain English so a partner, an office manager, or a junior associate can follow it without an agency in the loop. GBP setup, the 50-source citation list, the schema markup that actually moves the needle, the review request script, the location page template, and the audit checklist.

No upsell. No "book a discovery call." Buy it, run it, keep the difference between $97 and what you would have spent on a retainer.

Who's it for: solo firms, small partnerships, and anyone tired of paying for monthly reports that don't show retained clients.

#LawFirmMarketing #LocalSEO #LegalMarketing

topic: Why your Google Ads dashboard is lying to you — and what to look at instead
text:
Your Google Ads dashboard is built to make you feel productive, not profitable.

Clicks climbing. Impressions up. CTR holding. All easy to measure, easy to put in a status report, easy for an agency to take credit for.

None of them tell you whether you're paying for retained clients or paying for noise.

Google's incentive is to keep you spending. Your agency's incentive is to show movement. Yours is to grow a profitable firm. Those are three different incentives, and the dashboard only really serves the first two.

The metric that matters is cost per retained client by campaign. It's harder to pull. Nobody on your dashboard team will volunteer it.

That's the one to ask for.

What's the one number in your Ads dashboard you actually trust to predict revenue next quarter?

Full breakdown in comments 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #GoogleAds #MarketingROI

topic: The intake call your law firm missed today is now your competitor's client
text:
A common pattern: a law firm spends thousands a month on ads, then loses a third of inbound calls to voicemail.

Not intentionally. The phone rings during depositions, lunch, court appearances. The receptionist is juggling intake forms and walk-ins. Voicemails sit for four, six, twelve hours.

By the time someone calls back, the prospect signed with a competitor.

The ads worked. The website worked. The intake didn't.

When firms actually measure their answer rate and callback speed, the picture usually changes everything. The bottleneck isn't the marketing spend — it's what happens after the click.

The fix isn't always a new system. Sometimes it's just covering lunch hours, weekends, and the first 90 seconds of a call with discipline.

The conversion happens on the intake call. Not on the landing page. Not in the brochure.

How long does it currently take your firm to return a missed call from a prospect?

Full breakdown in comments 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #ClientAcquisition #LawFirmGrowth

topic: Firms that track cost-per-retained-client grow faster than firms that track cost-per-lead
text:
Cost per lead is the metric your agency loves and your bank account doesn't notice.

Here's the hierarchy that actually matters:

1. Cost per lead
Counts forms and calls. Says nothing about whether anyone signs.

2. Cost per qualified lead
Filters out tire-kickers. Closer to reality.

3. Cost per signed client
The first metric that touches revenue.

4. Cost per retained client
The only number that tells you if the channel funds the firm.

5. Lifetime value per client by source
Tells you what you can actually afford to spend at the top.

Pick one channel this week. Trace one campaign from spend to retained client. You'll know what to cut and what to scale.

Link in first comment 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #MarketingROI #LawFirmGrowth

topic: Most marketing agencies can't tell you their client's cost per retained client
text:
Most agencies report impressions, clicks, and lead counts. None of those answer whether the campaign is profitable for the firm.

Ask your agency on the next call: what is our cost per retained client, by channel?

If they can't answer in under a minute, what are you actually paying them to track?

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #MarketingROI

topic: Your SEO agency is optimizing for clicks. You need cases. Those are different metrics.
text:
Your SEO agency is winning. You're losing. Both can be true at the same time.

Rankings climb. Traffic climbs. The phone doesn't.

This is a common pattern. SEO contracts are written around metrics SEOs can control — keyword positions, organic sessions, page-one placements. None of those map directly to retained clients.

Keyword intent matters more than keyword volume. "What is a deposition" doesn't pay. "Best DUI attorney near me" pays. Those two terms need entirely different strategies, and most agencies don't differentiate.

It's not always bad faith. Most of the time it's a measurement problem the contract was never set up to solve.

The fix is rewriting the scoreboard. Tie SEO performance to leads from qualifying intent terms, not site traffic.

What's the gap right now between your traffic chart and your retained-client chart?

Full breakdown in comments 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #SEO #LawFirmGrowth

topic: The law firm marketing stack you actually need vs the one agencies sell you
text:
A common pattern: firms paying $3-5k a month on marketing tools they log into twice a quarter.

CRM, email platform, intake software, analytics dashboard, two SEO tools, a social scheduler. Everything onboarded with the agency that sold it. Nothing integrated. Nothing actually used to make decisions.

The data lives in silos. Decisions get made on gut feel anyway.

The firms that grow run leaner stacks. One CRM that touches every channel. One ad platform per network. One analytics layer that ties spend to signed clients. Everything else is justified by a specific use case or it gets cut.

The tool didn't grow the firm. The discipline of actually reading the data did.

Open your billing this week. Find the highest-cost subscription you can't remember the last time you logged into. Start there.

Which tool in your stack is doing real work, and which one is just there because someone said you needed it?

Full breakdown in comments 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #SmallBusiness #LawFirmGrowth

topic: Why "more leads" is almost always the wrong goal for law firms
text:
More leads is the wrong goal. Better leads is the goal.

Five things that separate firms running on lead volume from firms running on lead quality:

1. Volume
Fills the inbox. Burns the intake team. Tells you nothing about revenue.

2. Quality
Defined by case value and conversion probability. Worth more than volume by a wide margin.

3. Source mix
Referrals convert 5-10x better than cold ad clicks. Plan budget around that, not against it.

4. Filtering at the top
Good ads repel as much as they attract. Bad-fit prospects should self-select out.

5. Intake conversion
The one metric most firms never measure, and the one with the biggest leverage.

Pull last quarter's signed clients. Tag each one by source. The pattern usually surprises everyone in the room.

Link in first comment 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #ClientAcquisition #LawFirmGrowth

topic: CallRail set up wrong is worse than no CallRail
text:
CallRail installed and never reconciled is worse than no call tracking at all.

Numbers don't match the CRM. Tags drift over time. Source attribution gets messy. Decisions get made on data that quietly stopped being true six months ago.

When was the last time anyone at your firm pulled a CallRail report and matched it against actual signed clients?

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #CallTracking #MarketingROI

topic: Florida law firms: your competitors are bidding on your firm name in Google Ads right now
text:
Your competitor is bidding on your firm's name in Google right now. That's not unethical. That's marketing.

Branded keyword bidding is legal, common, and brutally effective. The prospect searching your firm by name has the highest intent of any lead you'll ever see — and right now, a competitor's ad is sitting above your organic listing.

This is a common pattern in competitive Florida markets. Most partners don't know it's happening until they search their own name on a phone that isn't logged into their Google account.

The fix is uncomfortable: bid on your own brand. Yes, you're paying for traffic that should already be yours. But Google decides what shows above the organic result, and Google isn't deciding in your favor unless you pay them.

The alternative is watching your own name route prospects to a competitor with a better Ads strategy.

When did you last search your own firm name in an incognito window to see what shows up?

Full breakdown in comments 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #GoogleAds #LawFirmGrowth

topic: The difference between a cheap client acquisition cost and an expensive one is usually your intake process
text:
The cheapest client you'll ever sign came from someone who breezed through your intake. The most expensive one is the lead you paid for and lost in the callback queue.

Firms pour budget into the top of the funnel. Ads. SEO. Content. Then the prospect lands on a 14-field contact form, a phone tree, and a callback window of 48 hours.

The expensive part already happened. Answering the phone is the cheap part.

The firms winning right now treat intake as a marketing channel, not an administrative task. Response in minutes. One clear next step. Friction stripped out wherever it can be.

Intake quality is the multiplier on every dollar you spend upstream. A great campaign with a broken intake produces a bad acquisition cost. A modest campaign with a tight intake produces a great one.

Fix intake before you touch the ads.

How many fields are on your contact form right now, and when did you last fill it out as a prospect would?

Full breakdown in comments 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #ClientAcquisition #LawFirmGrowth

topic: AI search (ChatGPT, Perplexity) is answering "best personal injury lawyer Tampa" right now
text:
Someone asked ChatGPT "best personal injury lawyer in Tampa" today. Your firm either showed up in the answer or it didn't.

What actually matters now:

1. AI search is current, not future
Clients are using it to choose lawyers right now, not in 2027.

2. The model summarizes, not links
Citations and mentions matter more than blue-link rankings.

3. Structured data tells the model who you are
Schema markup is no longer optional for local legal.

4. Reviews and third-party mentions tell the model you're trusted
This is the new backlink profile.

5. The defaults are getting set right now
Firms optimizing for AI search today become the ones models recommend in 18 months.

Ask ChatGPT for the best [your practice area] in [your city] this week. That answer is your scoreboard.

Link in first comment 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #AISearch #SEO

topic: Clio has the data to tell you exactly which cases came from which marketing channel
text:
Your case management system already knows which marketing channel sent your best last 50 clients. Almost no firm actually pulls that data.

The answer is sitting in Clio or whatever you use. You're just not reading it.

Pull your last 20 signed cases — can you state where each one came from, with data, in under an hour?

topic: Your marketing agency sends monthly reports. None of them mention cost per retained client. That is intentional.
text:
If your monthly marketing report shows impressions, clicks, and rankings — but not retained clients — your agency is reporting on their work, not yours.

Vanity metrics are easy to measure, easy to put in a deck, and almost impossible to argue with. That's the whole appeal. They give everyone something to point at.

Outcome metrics are messier. They lag. They require connecting marketing data to case management data. They occasionally embarrass the agency that produced the report.

That's exactly why outcome metrics are the ones worth tracking.

This doesn't mean top-of-funnel numbers are useless. They're leading indicators. The problem is when leading indicators become the whole report and the lagging indicators never show up.

Change the measurement and you change the agency's behavior. It's the fastest lever you have.

What's on your current monthly report that you actually use to make budget decisions?

Full breakdown in comments 👇

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #MarketingROI #LawFirmGrowth

topic: The law firm marketing audit most firms have never done — and what it usually reveals
text:
A pattern I see often: a firm spending six figures a year on marketing, and the partners can't name which channel brought their last five clients.

The budget got allocated by inertia. Ads kept running because they were running last year. The website redesign happened because someone said it was time. The content program is on autopilot. None of it actually measured against retained clients.

Nobody made a bad decision. Everyone just stopped asking the question.

A real audit starts at the outcome, not the campaign. Pull every signed client from the last 90 days. Trace each one back to first touch. The pattern that emerges is almost never what the marketing report suggested.

Some channels are doing all the work. Others are pure spend. A few are quietly cannibalizing referral traffic.

The outcome data tells the story. The campaign data doesn't.

If you had to cut half your marketing spend tomorrow, would you know with confidence which half?

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #MarketingROI #LawFirmGrowth

topic: Seven red flags that spell trouble with your law firm marketing agency
text:
I've spent 20+ years inside law firm marketing. In that time, I've reviewed hundreds of agency contracts and relationships. And I can tell you: the ones that fail don't die suddenly. They die by a thousand paper cuts.

It's not that a single project goes south. It's the patterns. The vanishing reports. The "we own your assets" clause buried in page 8. The account manager who can't make a single decision without someone you never meet.

These seven red flags have killed more law firm-agency partnerships than any single failure ever could. And the pattern they signal is almost always the same: misaligned incentives and eroding accountability.

If you've been asking yourself whether your current agency is the problem — or if you're just being impatient — this gives you a framework to diagnose what's actually happening.

#LawFirmMarketing #LegalMarketing #AgencyProblems
